title: How Much Does a Private ADHD Assessment Cost in the UK? 2026 Guide
meta_description: Private ADHD assessment costs in the UK range from £500-£1500. Compare prices, what's included, and factors affecting cost.
content:
How Much Does a Private ADHD Assessment Cost in the UK?

If you're considering a private ADHD assessment in the UK, cost is likely one of your first questions. With NHS waiting times stretching to 2-3 years in many areas, many people turn to private assessments as a faster alternative. Understanding the costs involved helps you plan your finances and find the right service for your needs.

Typical ADHD Assessment Costs

Private ADHD assessments in the UK typically range from £500 to £1,500 for a comprehensive diagnostic assessment. However, costs can vary significantly depending on location, the clinician's experience, and the complexity of your case.

  • Basic assessments: £500-£800 - Usually conducted by trainee psychiatrists or nurses under supervision
  • Standard assessments: £800-£1,200 - Carried out by experienced psychiatrists or ADHD specialists
  • Comprehensive assessments: £1,200-£1,500 - In-depth evaluations including psychological testing and detailed history taking

What's Included in the Assessment Cost?

The price of a private ADHD assessment typically covers several components:

  • Initial consultation and detailed medical history review
  • Psychological and cognitive testing
  • Structured interviews and rating scales
  • Review of school or childhood records (if available)
  • Feedback session with results and recommendations
  • Written diagnostic report
  • Guidance on next steps and treatment options

Factors Affecting Private ADHD Assessment Costs

Several factors influence the final price you'll pay for your assessment:

  • Clinician qualifications: Psychiatrists typically charge more than psychologists or nurses, reflecting their medical training and ability to prescribe medication
  • Location: London and other major cities tend to be more expensive. Rural areas may offer lower prices but fewer options
  • Experience and reputation: Well-established ADHD specialists with extensive experience may charge premium rates
  • Assessment comprehensiveness: More detailed assessments with additional testing cost more
  • Follow-up services: Some clinics include follow-up appointments or medication management in their fees
  • Clinic overheads: Private practices with premium locations charge more than community-based services

Is Private Assessment More Expensive Than NHS?

While private assessments have an upfront cost, they can actually be more cost-effective when you factor in time. Waiting 2-3 years for an NHS assessment means living with undiagnosed ADHD symptoms, which can impact work, relationships, and mental health. A private assessment allows you to get a diagnosis and start treatment quickly, potentially saving money in the long run through improved productivity and wellbeing.

Payment Options and Financing

Most private ADHD clinics offer flexible payment options:

  • Full payment upfront: Often comes with a small discount
  • Payment plans: Some clinics allow you to spread costs over multiple payments
  • Health insurance: Private health insurance may cover some costs - check your policy
  • Employee assistance schemes: Some employers offer EAP schemes that cover mental health assessments

Additional Costs to Consider

Beyond the assessment itself, you may face other expenses:

  • Travel costs: If your nearest clinic is far away, factor in travel expenses
  • Medication: If prescribed after diagnosis, ongoing medication costs apply (though ADHD medications are relatively affordable on the NHS)
  • Follow-up consultations: Some people need follow-up appointments for medication adjustment or support
  • Reports for employers/education: Additional copies of your diagnostic report may cost extra

Finding Affordable, Quality Assessments

To get good value for money, compare clinics in your area. Use our directory to find a clinic near you and review their pricing, qualifications, and patient feedback. Don't automatically choose the cheapest option - consider the clinician's experience, assessment comprehensiveness, and additional support offered.

Are Costs Covered by Insurance?

Some private health insurance policies cover ADHD assessments, though coverage varies widely. Check your policy details or contact your insurer before booking. If you're covered, you may only need to pay a small excess.

Summary

Private ADHD assessments in the UK cost between £500-£1,500, with most comprehensive assessments falling in the £800-£1,200 range. While this is an investment, getting a diagnosis quickly can transform your life by allowing you to access treatment and support that the NHS waiting list delays. Take time to research clinics, compare costs, and choose a qualified professional who offers good value for comprehensive care.

title: How Long Does an Autism Assessment Take? Timeline & Process
meta_description: Autism assessment timelines explained. From NHS referral to diagnosis, learn how long private assessments take vs NHS routes.
content:
How Long Does an Autism Assessment Take?

One of the biggest frustrations for people seeking an autism diagnosis in the UK is the sheer length of the process. NHS waiting lists can stretch to 3-4 years in some areas, leaving many adults uncertain and without support. If you're considering a private assessment, understanding the timeline helps you plan and set realistic expectations.

NHS Autism Assessment Timeline

The traditional NHS route for autism diagnosis involves several steps:

  • GP referral: 1-4 weeks (time to get a GP appointment)
  • Waiting list: 6 months to 4 years (varies dramatically by region)
  • Assessment appointments: 2-6 appointments over several months
  • Diagnostic report: 4-12 weeks after final appointment
  • Total time: 1-5 years from GP referral to diagnosis

Many regions report waiting times of 2-3 years simply to get your first appointment, and the assessment itself can take several months once it starts.

Private Autism Assessment Timeline

Private assessments are significantly faster:

  • Initial contact: Same day to 1 week to schedule
  • First appointment: 1-4 weeks from referral
  • Assessment duration: 1-3 appointments over 4-8 weeks
  • Diagnostic report: 1-4 weeks after final appointment
  • Total time: 6-12 weeks from initial contact to diagnosis

This represents a dramatic reduction - you could have a diagnosis in the time NHS patients wait for their first appointment.

The Single Assessment Appointment

Some private clinics offer single-appointment autism assessments lasting 2-3 hours. While faster, these assessments may be less comprehensive than multi-appointment evaluations. They work well for people with clear-cut presentations but may miss subtler cases, particularly in women and autistic people with lower support needs.

Multi-Appointment Comprehensive Assessments

More thorough private assessments typically involve multiple appointments:

  • Appointment 1 (1.5-2 hours): Initial consultation, detailed developmental history, medical and psychiatric history
  • Appointment 2 (1-2 hours): Formal autism diagnostic assessment tools (ADOS-2 or equivalent), observation, and interaction
  • Appointment 3 (30-60 mins): Results discussion, recommendations, and next steps

This approach typically takes 4-8 weeks from start to finish and provides a more thorough evaluation of your support needs.

Why Does NHS Assessment Take So Long?

NHS autism assessments involve multiple steps contributing to lengthy delays:

  • High demand: Limited diagnostic services face overwhelming referral numbers
  • Stricter criteria: Some NHS teams have higher diagnostic thresholds, requiring more evidence before diagnosis
  • Comprehensive assessment: NHS teams often conduct very thorough evaluations, involving collateral information from schools, employers, or family
  • Multi-disciplinary teams: Diagnosis may require input from psychiatrists, psychologists, and speech therapists, coordinating appointments takes time
  • Administrative delays: Bureaucracy in the NHS system adds weeks or months between steps

Factors Affecting Private Assessment Speed

While private assessments are faster overall, speed varies:

  • Clinician availability: Specialist psychiatrists may have longer waiting lists
  • Complexity of case: More complex presentations require more assessment time
  • Required additional testing: Some cases need psychological or speech/language assessment, extending timeline
  • Information gathering: Obtaining school records or family information takes time
  • Clinic efficiency: Well-organised clinics schedule faster than disorganised ones

Assessment Process Timeline Breakdown

Here's what typically happens week by week in a private assessment:

Week 1: You make initial contact and complete preliminary questionnaires. Basic clinical information is gathered.

Weeks 1-4: First appointment is scheduled. You prepare any documents (school reports, medical records) that help with assessment.

Weeks 4-8: First and subsequent appointments happen. You attend the clinic for assessment interviews and any standardised testing.

Weeks 8-12: The clinician writes your diagnostic report, compiling findings and conclusions.

Week 12+: You receive your report and attend feedback appointment to discuss diagnosis and next steps.

Expedited Assessments

Some private clinics offer expedited services for faster diagnosis:

  • Fast-track appointments: Prioritised scheduling within 1-2 weeks
  • Intensive assessment days: Complete assessment in a single day with multiple back-to-back sessions
  • Emergency referrals: For people in crisis (severe mental health concerns related to undiagnosed autism)

These services typically cost more but deliver results in 2-4 weeks.

After Diagnosis - What Happens Next?

Once you have your autism diagnosis, the next steps include:

  • NHS support: You can access NHS support services more easily with a diagnosis
  • Workplace adjustments: Your diagnosis helps you request reasonable adjustments at work
  • Education support: Students can access additional educational support with a diagnosis
  • Benefits: Diagnosis may make you eligible for disability benefits or discounts
  • Specialist support: You can access autism-specific support groups and services

Finding a Clinic That Fits Your Timeline

When choosing a private clinic, ask about their assessment timeline upfront. Use our directory to find a clinic near you and compare their typical wait times. Some clinics are faster than others, and if timeline is important to you, that should influence your choice.

Summary

Private autism assessments typically take 6-12 weeks from initial contact to diagnosis, compared to 1-5 years for NHS assessments. While faster, private assessments still require time for proper evaluation. The exact timeline depends on the clinic's efficiency, the complexity of your presentation, and the comprehensiveness of the assessment. When choosing a private provider, ask specifically about their typical timelines and choose a clinic that matches your needs and expectations.

title: Private vs NHS ADHD Assessment: Costs, Wait Times & Pros/Cons
meta_description: Compare private and NHS ADHD assessments. Waiting times, costs, and which route is right for you explained.
content:
Private vs NHS ADHD Assessment: Which Route is Right for You?

If you suspect you have ADHD, you have two main routes to diagnosis in the UK: the NHS and private assessment. Each has significant advantages and drawbacks. Understanding the differences helps you make the best choice for your situation.

NHS ADHD Assessment

The NHS route has been the traditional pathway for diagnosis, but it faces significant challenges:

  • Cost: Free at point of use (funded by your taxes)
  • Waiting time: 6 months to 3 years depending on region
  • Provider: NHS psychiatry or ADHD specialist clinics
  • Referral process: GP referral usually required; some regions allow self-referral
  • Diagnosis criteria: Strict NICE guidelines ensure consistent standards

Private ADHD Assessment

Private assessment offers an alternative with different benefits and trade-offs:

  • Cost: £500-£1,500 for a comprehensive assessment
  • Waiting time: 2-8 weeks from referral to diagnosis
  • Provider: Private psychiatrists, psychologists, or ADHD specialists
  • Referral process: Self-referral; GP letter helpful but not required
  • Diagnosis criteria: Similar to NHS (DSM-5 or ICD-11) but may vary by clinician

Cost Comparison

This is the most obvious difference between the two routes:

NHS: No upfront cost, fully funded by the NHS. However, you may have indirect costs (time off work for appointments, travel), and the long wait means living with undiagnosed ADHD for months or years.

Private: Typically £500-£1,500 upfront. However, faster diagnosis means you can start treatment and gain productivity improvements sooner, potentially offsetting the cost.

Wait Times: A Critical Difference

Perhaps the biggest difference is how quickly you get assessed:

  • NHS waiting lists: Many regions report waiting 2-3 years just for your first appointment. Some areas are even longer. After your appointment, the diagnostic process itself takes several months.
  • Private waiting lists: Most private clinics see patients within 2-8 weeks. This enormous time saving is a major advantage for people struggling with undiagnosed ADHD symptoms.

Quality of Assessment

Both routes aim to provide thorough, evidence-based assessments, but there are differences:

NHS: Advantages include standardised procedures, robust training, and multi-disciplinary input (psychiatrists, psychologists, nurses). Disadvantages include time pressure from high caseloads and sometimes limited appointment time.

Private: Advantages include more flexible appointment times and potentially more thorough assessment. Disadvantages include inconsistency in quality - you need to research the individual clinician's qualifications and experience carefully.

Medication After Diagnosis

An important consideration is whether the diagnosing clinician can prescribe medication:

NHS: Psychiatrists can prescribe ADHD medication. Psychologists cannot. Some NHS teams have specific protocols for medication initiation.

Private: Only psychiatrists can legally prescribe. If assessed by a private psychologist, you'll need a separate psychiatry appointment to get medication. Some private clinics provide integrated services where psychiatrists are available for medication management.

Follow-up Care After Diagnosis

What happens after you get your diagnosis matters:

NHS: If diagnosed privately, many NHS teams will still provide medication management and ongoing support on the NHS. However, they may have long waits for these services too. Some NHS teams are reluctant to take over care from private assessments.

Private: Some private clinics offer ongoing medication support; others don't. You'll need to discuss follow-up arrangements before your assessment.

Right to Choose

An important option is NHS Right to Choose. If your GP refers you to the NHS but the local waiting list exceeds 13 weeks, you can choose a different NHS provider (often private ADHD clinics commissioned by the NHS). This can combine NHS funding with faster private assessment - a major advantage if you qualify.

When to Choose NHS

The NHS route makes sense if:

  • You have a very tight budget and cannot afford private assessment
  • Your local NHS waiting list is short (under 6 months)
  • You want comprehensive follow-up medication support integrated with NHS care
  • You don't mind waiting for a diagnosis while living with symptoms

When to Choose Private

Private assessment makes sense if:

  • Your symptoms significantly impact your work or wellbeing
  • You can afford the £500-£1,500 cost
  • You want a diagnosis quickly (within weeks, not years)
  • Your NHS waiting list is very long (over 6 months)
  • You want more choice in clinician or assessment style

Hybrid Approach

Many people use a hybrid approach:

  1. Get assessed privately for a fast diagnosis and to start treatment
  2. Involve your GP in your care from the start
  3. Ask the NHS to take over medication management once diagnosed

This combines the speed of private assessment with NHS support for ongoing care.

Finding the Right Private Clinic

If you choose the private route, quality matters. Use our directory to find a clinic near you with experienced, qualified clinicians. Check their qualifications (they should be a registered psychiatrist or psychologist with ADHD specialism), read patient reviews, and ask about their assessment process before committing.

Summary

The NHS route is free but faces extremely long waiting times that can delay diagnosis by years. Private assessment costs money upfront but delivers diagnosis within weeks and gives you more choice and control. For most people struggling with undiagnosed ADHD, private assessment is worth the investment in improved quality of life. However, if you can access NHS Right to Choose or have a short NHS waiting list, that might be your best option. Whatever you choose, getting a diagnosis is the crucial first step toward treatment and support.

title: What to Expect During an Adult ADHD Assessment: Step-by-Step
meta_description: Detailed guide to what happens during adult ADHD assessment. Interviews, tests, questions to prepare for in the UK.
content:
What to Expect During an Adult ADHD Assessment

If you're about to have an ADHD assessment, you probably have questions about what will happen. Understanding the process helps you feel more confident and prepared. Here's a detailed walkthrough of what to expect during a typical adult ADHD assessment in the UK.

Before Your Assessment: Preparation

Several things happen before you arrive at your appointment:

Initial questionnaire: Most clinics send you screening questionnaires to complete before your appointment. These include the ASRS (Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale) or similar tools. Complete these honestly and thoroughly - they help the clinician understand your experiences and focus the assessment.

Information gathering: Gather documents that support your assessment. School reports, university records, previous psychological assessments, employment records, or medical history can all be helpful. The clinician will ask about your childhood and how your symptoms have progressed, so childhood evidence is particularly valuable.

Identify a collateral source: If possible, arrange for a family member or close friend to provide information about how ADHD affects you. Some clinics use a collateral questionnaire to gather this information.

The Initial Appointment

Your first assessment appointment typically lasts 1.5-2 hours. Here's what usually happens:

Welcome and Explanation

You'll meet your clinician, who will explain the assessment process, answer any questions, and discuss confidentiality. This is also when you'll discuss fees and next steps.

Developmental History

Expect detailed questions about your childhood. The clinician will ask:

  • Your early developmental milestones (when you walked, talked, potty trained)
  • Your school experience - did you struggle academically, socially, or behaviourally?
  • Whether anyone mentioned ADHD, concentration difficulties, or behaviour problems when you were young
  • Your family history - do relatives have ADHD, anxiety, depression, or other neurodevelopmental conditions?
  • Whether you were ever assessed or diagnosed with anything as a child

This is crucial because ADHD must have been present since childhood to diagnose it in adults. Be honest about your school struggles, even if you were never formally assessed.

Current Symptoms

The clinician will discuss how ADHD affects you now:

  • Do you struggle with focus and concentration?
  • Do you start tasks but don't finish them?
  • Are you disorganised? Do you lose things, forget appointments, miss deadlines?
  • Do you interrupt others or struggle to wait your turn?
  • Do you feel restless or fidgety?
  • Do you procrastinate or struggle with time management?
  • How do these symptoms affect work, relationships, and daily life?

Be specific with examples. Instead of "I'm disorganised," say "I can't keep my desk tidy, I lose important documents, and I've missed two work deadlines this year because I forgot about them."

Medical and Psychiatric History

You'll be asked about:

  • Any physical health conditions (thyroid problems, heart conditions, epilepsy can mimic ADHD)
  • Medications you take
  • Allergies
  • Mental health diagnoses (anxiety, depression, bipolar disorder often coexist with ADHD)
  • Substance use (alcohol, drugs, caffeine habits)
  • Sleep patterns and quality
  • Significant life events or trauma

Standardised Assessment Tools

Your clinician will use standardised questionnaires and rating scales:

  • ASRS-v1.1: Six questions screening for ADHD symptoms
  • CAARS (Conners Adult ADHD Rating Scales): Comprehensive assessment of current ADHD symptoms
  • Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in Adults (DIVA): Structured interview about childhood and current symptoms
  • WURS (Wender Utah Rating Scale): Focuses on childhood ADHD symptoms

These aren't tests you can pass or fail - they're tools to standardise assessment and ensure nothing is missed.

Observational Assessment

Throughout the appointment, the clinician observes:

  • Whether you fidget, move about, or struggle to sit still
  • Whether you're easily distracted or have difficulty focusing on the conversation
  • Your speech - do you talk very fast or interrupt?
  • Your eye contact and engagement
  • Your organisation and punctuality (were you on time?)
  • Your emotional regulation

Don't worry about being observed - the clinician understands that your nervousness or best behaviour during the appointment isn't necessarily how you are day-to-day.

Cognitive Assessment (Sometimes)

Some clinics include cognitive or IQ testing to assess:

  • Your working memory (holding information temporarily)
  • Processing speed
  • Attention and concentration
  • Overall cognitive functioning

These tests involve puzzles, pattern recognition, reading, and listening tasks. They're not intelligence tests in the traditional sense but assess different cognitive functions. They take 30-90 minutes depending on the clinic.

Between Appointments

If your assessment involves multiple appointments, you may be asked to:

  • Complete additional questionnaires for a family member or close friend
  • Keep an ADHD diary noting concentration, impulsivity, and organisational issues
  • Return with additional documents or information
  • Think about specific examples of how ADHD affects you to discuss further

Second Appointment (if applicable)

If your clinic uses multiple appointments, the second session typically focuses on:

  • Going deeper into specific areas identified in the first appointment
  • Detailed discussion of symptoms across different contexts (home, work, relationships)
  • Review of additional documents or collateral information
  • Clarification of any ambiguous areas

The Feedback Appointment

Once assessment is complete, you'll meet with the clinician to discuss results:

If ADHD is diagnosed: The clinician will explain your specific ADHD presentation (inattentive type, hyperactive-impulsive type, or combined). They'll discuss which symptoms are most prominent and how they manifest in your life.

If ADHD isn't diagnosed: They'll explain what they found instead. Sometimes what presents as ADHD is actually anxiety, depression, sleep problems, or other conditions. The clinician will discuss what this means and next steps for support.

Medication discussion: If diagnosed and interested in medication, the clinician will discuss options (methylphenidate, amphetamine-based stimulants, atomoxetine, or guanfacine). They'll explain effects, side effects, and how medication is monitored.

Practical recommendations: The clinician will suggest practical strategies and support for managing ADHD.

Your Written Report

After your assessment, you'll receive a detailed written report including:

  • Summary of your history and current symptoms
  • Assessment findings and test results
  • Diagnostic conclusion (with or without ADHD diagnosis)
  • Recommendations for treatment and support
  • Information about next steps and follow-up care

This report is yours to keep and can be shared with your GP, employer, or educational institution.

After Your Assessment

Once you have your diagnosis and report, your next steps might include:

  • Medication management: If prescribed medication, regular appointments with your clinician or GP to monitor effects and adjust doses
  • Occupational therapy: Some people benefit from support with organisation, time management, and executive function strategies
  • Talking therapies: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) or coaching can help manage ADHD
  • Workplace adjustments: Your diagnosis helps you request reasonable adjustments at work
  • GP involvement: Share your report with your GP so they can support your care

Tips for a Smooth Assessment

  • Be honest: The clinician needs the true picture, not what you think they want to hear
  • Give specific examples: Don't just say "I'm forgetful" - describe specific times you forgot important things
  • Allow time: Don't rush to other commitments - assessments take several hours
  • Bring documents: School reports, medical records, and anything documenting your history
  • Get informed consent: Understand what you're agreeing to and how your information will be used
  • Ask questions: If anything is unclear, ask for explanation

Finding a Quality Clinic

Ensure your assessment is thorough and professional by choosing a reputable clinic. Use our directory to find a clinic near you with experienced, qualified clinicians. Ask about their assessment process, qualifications, and experience with adult ADHD before committing.

Summary

A typical adult ADHD assessment involves detailed questions about your childhood and current symptoms, standardised rating scales, and sometimes cognitive testing. It's a collaborative process aimed at understanding your experiences and making an accurate diagnosis. Going into your assessment informed about what to expect helps you feel more confident and ensures you provide the information clinicians need to assess you accurately.

title: Signs of Autism in Adults: When to Seek an Assessment
meta_description: Recognise signs of autism in adults. Common traits, late diagnosis, and when to seek assessment in the UK.
content:
Signs of Autism in Adults: When to Seek an Assessment

Autism spectrum disorder in adults is often missed or diagnosed only in later life. Many autistic adults were never identified as children and have spent their lives feeling different, struggling socially, or being misdiagnosed with other conditions. If you recognise yourself in this pattern, you're not alone - and seeking an assessment can be transformative.

Why Late Autism Diagnosis is Common

Many intelligent, capable adults go undiagnosed because:

  • Masking: Autistic people often learn to mask their autism by mimicking non-autistic behaviour. This is exhausting but effective at hiding autism in social situations.
  • Girls and women: Autism presents differently in females and was historically underdiagnosed. Many girls weren't identified because their autism looked different from boys'.
  • Different thresholds: You may have been seen as shy, awkward, or having social difficulties without anyone suggesting autism.
  • Less obvious presentation: Some autistic people have subtle traits that aren't immediately obvious, especially if they have average or high intelligence.
  • No major problems in childhood: If you coped reasonably well as a child, diagnosis was less likely.

Core Traits of Autism in Adults

Autism has two main areas of difficulty, plus associated traits:

Social Communication Difficulties

Autistic adults often experience:

  • Understanding social rules: Social situations feel confusing. You might struggle with small talk, understand jokes less readily, or miss social cues (like when someone is upset or bored).
  • Reading facial expressions: You find it difficult to interpret others' expressions and emotions, preferring explicit verbal communication.
  • Eye contact: Maintaining eye contact feels uncomfortable or unnatural. You might not look at people when talking or listening.
  • Friendship difficulties: You struggle to make friends, maintain friendships, or understand social hierarchies. You might have few close friends or feel isolated despite being around people.
  • Communication style: Your speech might be formal, unusual in rhythm, or very literal. You might struggle with give-and-take in conversations.
  • Understanding others' perspectives: Imagining what someone else is thinking or feeling is difficult. Their behaviour often surprises or confuses you.

Restricted or Repetitive Interests and Behaviours

Autistic adults often have:

  • Intense, focused interests: You have deep, passionate interests that consume much of your time and mental energy. You know vast amounts of detail about your interests.
  • Need for routine and sameness: Change is distressing. You prefer predictable routines, familiar places, and knowing what to expect.
  • Repetitive behaviours: You engage in repetitive movements (hand flapping, rocking, leg bouncing) when stressed or excited, often without realising it.
  • Stimming: You use sensory stimulation to regulate (fidget toys, listening to music repeatedly, arranging objects).
  • Rigid thinking: You think in logical, rule-based ways. When rules change without explanation, you find it distressing.
  • Resistance to change: Changes to routines, unexpected events, or transitions between activities cause anxiety.

Sensory Sensitivities

Many autistic people have heightened sensory sensitivities:

  • Sound sensitivity: Certain sounds (traffic, loud voices, alarms, or fluorescent lights) cause discomfort or pain. You might use earplugs or noise-cancelling headphones.
  • Light sensitivity: Bright lights, flickering lights (like fluorescent bulbs), or too much visual stimulation feel overwhelming.
  • Texture sensitivity: Certain textures feel unbearable (clothing tags, seams, particular fabrics). You might wear only specific clothing types.
  • Smell and taste sensitivity: Strong smells are overwhelming. Your diet might be restricted to certain textures or tastes.
  • Touch sensitivity: You might dislike being touched, even by close family. Hugs, handshakes, or unexpected touch feel invasive.
  • Sensory seeking: Alternatively, you might seek intense sensory input (spinning, jumping, very spicy food).

Associated Traits and Difficulties

Autistic people commonly experience:

  • Anxiety: Particularly social anxiety, generalised anxiety, or anxiety triggered by uncertainty and sensory overwhelm
  • Executive function difficulties: Struggling with organisation, planning, time management, and starting tasks
  • Emotional regulation: Difficulty controlling emotional responses; emotions can feel overwhelming or very intense
  • Depression: High rates of depression, partly due to masking and the stress of living in a non-autistic world
  • Sleep difficulties: Many autistic people struggle with sleep onset or quality
  • Perfectionism: Setting high standards for yourself and becoming distressed when things aren't perfect
  • Burnout: Following periods of stress, masking, or sensory overload, you might experience autistic burnout - a state of physical, cognitive, and emotional exhaustion

How Autism Presents Differently in Women and Girls

Autism in females is often missed because:

  • Better masking: Girls often learn to hide autism better than boys, appearing more socially capable than they actually are
  • Different interests: Female special interests might be more socially acceptable (animals, books) rather than obvious (trains, computers)
  • Internalised difficulties: Girls' autism often manifests as anxiety or depression rather than obvious behavioural problems
  • Later puberty difficulties: Some girls cope okay until puberty, when social demands increase dramatically and stress builds
  • Quiet presentation: Girls might be quiet and anxious rather than disruptive, making autism less obvious

Red Flags You Might Be Autistic

Consider seeking assessment if you identify with several of these:

  • You've felt "different" from your peers your whole life but no one could explain why
  • You struggle with making or maintaining friendships despite wanting them
  • Social situations feel exhausting and you need significant recovery time afterward
  • You have intense, absorbing interests that dominate your life
  • You're bothered by sensory things that don't bother others (sounds, lights, textures)
  • You prefer things to be predictable and struggle with change
  • You've been diagnosed with depression or anxiety that didn't fully respond to treatment
  • You think in literal, logical ways and struggle with idioms or implied meanings
  • You spend significant energy pretending to be someone you're not in social situations
  • You have relatively few close friendships but those friendships are very intense
  • You struggle with executive function (organisation, planning, task initiation)
  • You experienced burnout after periods of stress that seemed manageable to others

Autism vs. Other Conditions

Autism is sometimes confused with or occurs alongside other conditions:

ADHD: Overlap exists - many autistic people also have ADHD. Key difference: ADHD is about attention and impulse control, while autism is about social communication and restricted interests.

Anxiety: Autistic people often have anxiety, but anxiety alone doesn't explain autism traits. Autism is lifelong; anxiety is a symptom that can be treated.

Personality disorders: Sometimes autism is mistakenly diagnosed as personality disorder. Key difference: Autism is developmental; personality disorders involve relationship patterns that cause distress to others.

Introversion: Autistic traits are more pervasive than introversion. Introverts still understand social rules; many autistic people genuinely don't.

Why Getting an Assessment Matters

If you suspect you're autistic, seeking assessment offers real benefits:

  • Self-understanding: Finally understanding why you've struggled socially, sensorially, or with certain tasks is validating and relieving
  • Access to support: A diagnosis opens doors to accommodations at work, education, and in daily life
  • Mental health improvement: Understanding yourself reduces shame and self-blame, often improving anxiety and depression
  • Better relationships: You can communicate your needs to partners, family, and friends
  • Workplace adjustments: Employers must provide reasonable adjustments for diagnosed disabilities
  • Access to services: Some autistic-specific support is only available to diagnosed individuals

Finding a Clinic for Assessment

If you've recognised yourself in these descriptions, the next step is seeking an assessment. Use our directory to find a clinic near you offering adult autism assessment. Look for clinicians with experience in adult autism, particularly female/non-binary diagnosis, as this is a growing area of specialisation.

What to Expect in Assessment

An autism assessment typically involves detailed questions about your developmental history, current traits, social experiences, and sensory sensitivities. You might complete questionnaires like the Autism Quotient (AQ) or Autism Spectrum Quotient (ASQ). Some assessments include an ADOS-2 (Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule), which is a structured interaction with the clinician to observe autistic traits. The assessment aims to understand whether your difficulties match autism criteria.

Summary

Autism in adults often goes unrecognised, especially in women and people who've become skilled at masking. If you've struggled with social communication, sensory sensitivity, or intense interests throughout your life; if you feel you've never quite fit in despite being intelligent and capable; or if you're concerned that you might be autistic, seeking an assessment is worthwhile. A diagnosis can bring understanding, validation, and access to support that transforms your life. Take the first step by exploring assessment options in your area through our directory.

title: ADHD Medication After Diagnosis: What to Expect & Options
meta_description: Guide to ADHD medication after diagnosis. Medication types, effects, side effects, and how to work with your prescriber.
content:
ADHD Medication After Diagnosis: Options and What to Expect

Getting an ADHD diagnosis is an important step, but it's just the beginning. The next question many people ask is about medication. Will I be prescribed something? What are my options? What are the risks and benefits? Understanding ADHD medication helps you make informed decisions about your treatment.

Do I Need Medication?

Medication isn't mandatory after diagnosis. Some people manage ADHD effectively through lifestyle changes, workplace adjustments, and coaching. However, many people find medication genuinely transformative - helping them focus, organise, and manage impulses in ways they couldn't before.

The decision to take medication should be made collaboratively with your clinician, considering:

  • Severity of your ADHD symptoms
  • Impact on your work, relationships, and wellbeing
  • Your personal preferences and concerns
  • Medical history and potential contraindications
  • Previous medication responses

Main ADHD Medications

Several medications are licenced for ADHD in the UK. They fall into two main categories: stimulants and non-stimulants.

Stimulant Medications

Stimulants work by increasing dopamine and noradrenaline in the brain, improving focus, motivation, and impulse control.

Methylphenidate (Ritalin, Concerta, Medikinet)

  • How it works: Blocks reuptake of dopamine and noradrenaline, making these neurochemicals more available in your brain
  • Forms: Immediate-release (Ritalin - lasts 3-4 hours), extended-release (Concerta, Medikinet - last 8-12 hours)
  • Typical dose: 10-60mg daily for adults, divided into multiple doses or one long-acting dose
  • Onset: Effects appear within 20-30 minutes for immediate-release, 1-2 hours for extended-release
  • Effectiveness: Works well for 60-80% of people with ADHD
  • Common side effects: Reduced appetite, insomnia, headaches, jitteriness, increased heart rate

Amphetamine-based medications (Adderall, Elvanse)

  • How they work: Similar to methylphenidate, but slightly different mechanism. Increase dopamine and noradrenaline release
  • Forms: Immediate-release and extended-release (Elvanse) options
  • Typical dose: 10-40mg daily, adjusted based on response
  • Onset: 30 minutes to 1 hour
  • Effectiveness: Effective for 70-80% of ADHD sufferers; sometimes works when methylphenidate doesn't
  • Common side effects: Similar to methylphenidate; appetite suppression often more pronounced
  • UK availability: Less commonly prescribed than methylphenidate in UK but becoming more available

Non-Stimulant Medications

Non-stimulants work differently and may be preferred by people who can't take stimulants or experience intolerable side effects.

Atomoxetine (Strattera)

  • How it works: Selective noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor (SNRI). Increases noradrenaline availability in the brain
  • Typical dose: 40-100mg daily, given as a single dose
  • Onset: 2-4 weeks to feel full effects (slower than stimulants)
  • Effectiveness: Effective for 60-70% of ADHD sufferers
  • Advantages: Not controlled drug; lower abuse potential; works steadily throughout day
  • Disadvantages: Slower onset; may cause nausea initially
  • Common side effects: Nausea, decreased appetite, dizziness, fatigue

Guanfacine (Intuniv)

  • How it works: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist. Affects different brain circuits than stimulants
  • Typical dose: 1-4mg daily
  • Onset: Several weeks
  • Effectiveness: Effective for 50-60% of people with ADHD
  • Advantages: Lower abuse potential; works for people who don't respond to stimulants
  • Disadvantages: Slower onset; less robust effects than stimulants
  • Common side effects: Drowsiness, headaches, fatigue, dizziness

How Stimulants Work

ADHD involves insufficient dopamine and noradrenaline in key brain regions responsible for attention, motivation, and impulse control. Stimulant medications increase these neurotransmitters, allowing better signal transmission. This helps:

  • Improve focus and concentration
  • Increase motivation and follow-through on tasks
  • Reduce impulsive behaviours
  • Enhance working memory
  • Improve organisation and planning

Medication Trial and Adjustment

Finding the right medication and dose usually takes time:

Week 1: Start on lowest dose. Your clinician will discuss when to take it and what to expect.

Week 2-4: Assess response. Keep a diary of effects, side effects, and how you feel. Some side effects (jitteriness, nausea) often improve as your body adjusts.

Follow-up appointment: Discuss your experience. If not working well, your clinician might increase dose, change timing, or try a different medication.

Ongoing monitoring: Once stable, regular appointments (monthly initially, then every 3-6 months) monitor effects and check side effects, blood pressure, and heart rate.

Expected Effects

When ADHD medication is working well, people often report:

  • "Brain fog" lifting - thinking feels clearer
  • Easier to start tasks - procrastination reduces
  • Better focus - fewer distractions pull your attention away
  • Improved time awareness - less time blindness
  • More impulse control - easier to think before speaking or acting
  • Better organisation - managing multiple tasks becomes possible
  • Reduced mental fatigue from compensating for ADHD

It's important to note that medication doesn't give superpowers. It brings you closer to how non-ADHD people naturally function. It helps you use your intelligence and skills more effectively.

Common Side Effects

Stimulant side effects are usually manageable and often temporary:

  • Appetite suppression: Eat regular meals even if not hungry. Protein-heavy foods help. Many people need reminders to eat
  • Insomnia: Avoid taking medication late in the day. Establish good sleep hygiene
  • Jitteriness: Often improves with time or lower doses. Caffeine may worsen this
  • Headaches: Usually temporary; stay hydrated
  • Increased heart rate/blood pressure: Monitored at appointments. Significant increases warrant dosage adjustment or medication change
  • Mood changes: Some people report increased anxiety or mood swings; discuss with clinician

Medication Monitoring

When taking ADHD medication, your clinician will monitor:

  • Response: Is it working? Are you seeing improvements in focus, impulse control, organisation?
  • Side effects: What side effects are you experiencing? Are they manageable?
  • Physical health: Blood pressure and heart rate checked regularly. Some people need ECG monitoring
  • Mental health: Stimulants can unmask or worsen underlying anxiety or mood problems
  • Dose adequacy: Is the current dose optimal? Can it be reduced? Does it need increasing?

What About Addiction and Abuse?

Stimulant medications have abuse potential, which concerns many people. However, research shows that when taken as prescribed for ADHD, addiction risk is very low. People with untreated ADHD have higher substance abuse risk overall, suggesting that appropriate treatment actually reduces risk. Responsible prescribing (regular monitoring, prescription controls, patient education) minimises any risk.

Long-term Medication Use

Many people take ADHD medication long-term - years or decades - with good safety profiles. Some considerations:

  • Breaks from medication: Some people take drug-free days or weekends. Discuss this with your clinician rather than stopping suddenly
  • Tolerance: Some people find medication becomes less effective over time, requiring dose adjustment
  • Life changes: You may need to adjust medication when work patterns, sleep schedules, or stress levels change
  • Regular reviews: Annual reviews assess whether medication is still needed and working well

Non-Medication Approaches

Medication is most effective combined with other strategies:

  • Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT): Teaches strategies for managing ADHD symptoms
  • ADHD coaching: Practical help with organisation, time management, and executive function
  • Lifestyle changes: Good sleep, exercise, reducing stress, structured routines
  • Workplace adjustments: Remote work, flexible hours, reduced distractions, task breakdown
  • Technology aids: Reminders, alarms, apps for organisation

Getting Medication Through Your Clinic

If your private clinic clinician is a psychiatrist, they can prescribe your medication directly. Some private clinicians prefer to refer you to your GP for ongoing prescribing once diagnosis is made. Either way, your diagnosis gives you evidence for your GP to support your ADHD treatment. Use our directory to find a clinic that offers integrated medication management if ongoing prescribing support is important to you.

Summary

ADHD medication, particularly stimulants, can be transformative when the right medication at the right dose is found. The process involves careful selection, trial, monitoring, and adjustment. While side effects occur, they're usually manageable and often temporary. Medication works best combined with lifestyle changes and psychological approaches. If you've been diagnosed with ADHD and are considering medication, discuss the options thoroughly with your clinician to understand what might work best for your particular presentation and life circumstances.

title: NHS Right to Choose for ADHD: Getting Fast Assessment on the NHS
meta_description: Explained: NHS Right to Choose for ADHD. Get private assessment funded by NHS when waiting lists exceed 13 weeks.
content:
NHS Right to Choose for ADHD: Your Right to Fast Assessment

If you've been waiting for NHS ADHD assessment and your local waiting list is very long, you have a right that could dramatically speed up your diagnosis. NHS Right to Choose allows you to be assessed at a different provider - often a private ADHD clinic - with the NHS funding the cost. Understanding this right could be the key to getting diagnosed quickly without paying thousands out of pocket.

What Is NHS Right to Choose?

Right to Choose is an NHS right that says if you've been on a waiting list for a non-emergency referral for more than 13 weeks (approximately 3 months), you can ask to be seen by a different provider. This "different provider" is often a private ADHD clinic commissioned by the NHS to provide assessments.

In practice, this means you might be assessed at a private clinic with exactly the same diagnostic standards as the NHS, but you don't pay the £500-£1,500 cost yourself. The NHS funds it.

How Does Right to Choose Work?

The process has several steps:

Step 1: Get on the NHS waiting list

You must first be referred to the NHS. This happens when:

  • Your GP refers you to your local NHS ADHD service, OR
  • You're already on the NHS waiting list for ADHD assessment

If you haven't already been referred to the NHS, start by asking your GP for a referral. They should refer you to your local ADHD diagnostic service. Most GPs will do this if you explain you think you have ADHD.

Step 2: Check you're eligible for Right to Choose

You qualify for Right to Choose if:

  • You've been on the waiting list for more than 13 weeks (about 3 months)
  • You're waiting for a routine (non-urgent) referral
  • You're being treated by the NHS (either as an NHS patient or a funded patient)
  • There are other providers available in your area

Most people waiting for ADHD assessment qualify - 13 weeks is a short wait compared to NHS ADHD waiting lists, which often exceed 2-3 years.

Step 3: Ask for Right to Choose

Contact your GP or the NHS service you're waiting for and ask: "I've been waiting for more than 13 weeks. I'd like to exercise my Right to Choose and be assessed elsewhere." They should provide you with a list of approved providers in your area or region.

If they're reluctant, be persistent. Right to Choose is your legal right, and NHS staff should facilitate it even if they're busy or would prefer you to wait.

Step 4: Choose a provider

You'll be given options of private ADHD clinics commissioned by the NHS. Compare them based on:

  • Location and accessibility
  • Waiting time (different providers have different availability)
  • Clinician experience
  • Patient feedback if available
  • Whether they offer medication management after diagnosis

You can use our directory to research providers and find one that meets your needs. If you're given a limited list, you can ask whether other providers are available.

Step 5: Contact your chosen provider

Once you've chosen, the NHS initiates a referral to that provider. The private clinic will then contact you to arrange your assessment. Some providers are faster than others - waiting times typically range from 4 weeks to several months, depending on availability.

Step 6: Get assessed

You'll be assessed through the private clinic's normal process. The key difference is that the NHS is paying, not you. There may be very minor differences (the clinic might be slightly less comfortable than NHS settings), but the diagnostic quality should be equivalent.

Step 7: Get your diagnosis and report

Once assessed and diagnosed, you'll receive your diagnostic report from the private clinic. You should also be registered with the clinic for any follow-up care or medication management they provide.

What's Covered by Right to Choose?

The NHS typically funds:

  • The assessment itself (typically £1,000-£1,500 worth of service)
  • The diagnostic interview and testing
  • Your diagnostic report

What's usually NOT covered:

  • Ongoing medication management beyond initial diagnosis
  • Therapy or coaching
  • Additional reports or copies of your report for employers/education

Ask your chosen provider what exactly is included in their Right to Choose assessment to avoid unexpected costs.

Benefits of Using Right to Choose

  • No cost: Huge financial saving - you get a private assessment funded by the NHS
  • Fast assessment: Instead of waiting 2-3 years, you're typically assessed within 2-6 months
  • Choice: You choose your provider rather than accepting whoever your local NHS service assigns
  • Quality assessment: Private clinics commissioned by the NHS meet quality standards and are regulated
  • Quicker treatment: With diagnosis months earlier, you can start medication and support sooner

Potential Challenges

While Right to Choose is excellent, some challenges can arise:

  • Limited provider choice: You might have only a few options in your area. Sometimes the only options are far away.
  • NHS staff resistance: Some NHS staff are reluctant about Right to Choose and might not proactively offer it. You may need to be assertive.
  • Travel distance: If you're in a rural area, your options might be in cities requiring significant travel.
  • Referral delays: Even after choosing a provider, NHS administration can take several weeks to process the referral.
  • Post-diagnosis gap: If the private clinic doesn't offer medication management, there can be a gap between diagnosis and being able to start treatment with your GP

Medication After Right to Choose Assessment

Once diagnosed through Right to Choose, medication works like this:

  • If the private clinic prescribes: They might initiate ADHD medication. You'll need to discuss ongoing prescribing arrangements - will the NHS take over, or will the clinic continue?
  • If the private clinic doesn't prescribe: You'll need to go back to your GP with your diagnosis report and ask them to prescribe. Many GPs will do this, though some are cautious about taking over from private prescribers.

Ideally, choose a Right to Choose provider that offers medication management as part of their service, ensuring continuity of care.

Right to Choose vs. Waiting for NHS

Scenarios where Right to Choose is worth using:

  • Your NHS waiting list is more than 6 months
  • Your symptoms are significantly impacting your work or wellbeing
  • You want faster diagnosis and earlier access to treatment
  • You have good provider options available

Scenarios where NHS waiting might be okay:

  • Your local NHS waiting list is relatively short (under 6 months)
  • You prefer integrated NHS follow-up care
  • Travelling to a private clinic isn't feasible
  • You're coping reasonably well while waiting

Important: NHS Right to Choose vs. Private Assessment

Don't confuse Right to Choose with private assessment. When using Right to Choose:

  • The NHS funds the assessment
  • You don't pay
  • The clinic is commissioned by the NHS to specific standards
  • Your diagnosis feeds into NHS records

When paying privately:

  • You pay the full £500-£1,500
  • You choose any clinic you want (not just NHS-commissioned ones)
  • The diagnosis is your property; you decide whether to share with NHS

How to Exercise Your Right to Choose

If you want to use Right to Choose, here's a template email to send your GP or NHS service:

"I was referred to [local NHS ADHD service] on [date]. I understand I've now been waiting for more than 13 weeks. I would like to exercise my Right to Choose. Please provide me with a list of alternative providers, so I can select one for my ADHD assessment."

If they don't respond within a reasonable timeframe (2-4 weeks), follow up by phone.

Finding a Right to Choose Provider

Your NHS service should give you a list of available providers, but you can research further using our directory. Look for clinics in your area or nearby that are NHS-commissioned and see what others say about them. Many private ADHD clinics work with the NHS through Right to Choose arrangements.

Summary

NHS Right to Choose is a powerful, underutilised right that lets you access fast private assessment funded by the NHS when waiting lists exceed 13 weeks. For most people waiting for ADHD diagnosis, this is a game-changer - turning a 2-3 year wait into 2-6 months with no cost. If you're on an NHS waiting list, ask about Right to Choose. You have nothing to lose and months of faster diagnosis to gain. Use our directory to research potential providers and make an informed choice about where you'd like to be assessed.

title: How to Prepare for Autism Assessment: What to Bring & Tips
meta_description: Prepare for autism assessment. Documents to gather, questions to ask, what to expect, and tips for success.
content:
How to Prepare for Your Autism Assessment

Getting ready for an autism assessment helps you make the most of your appointment and gives the clinician the information they need for an accurate diagnosis. Preparation also reduces anxiety by helping you know what to expect. Here's a comprehensive guide to preparing for your assessment.

Gather Important Documents

Bringing supporting documents helps your clinician understand your history and makes the assessment smoother.

School Records

School reports are invaluable because they document your childhood functioning:

  • Primary school reports: Look for any mention of social difficulties, unusual interests, sensory sensitivities, or learning differences
  • Secondary school reports: Changes in social functioning, achievement vs. potential, behaviour issues, friendships
  • Teacher comments: Specific observations about how you functioned in classroom and social settings
  • Special educational needs (SEN) paperwork: If you had an Education, Health and Care Plan (EHCP) or were assessed for learning difficulties

If you don't have physical reports, contact your old schools. They may keep records for 7-10 years. Many schools will email or post copies if you request them, though some charge a small fee.

Medical Records

Ask your GP surgery for a printout of your medical history. Include:

  • Any mention of developmental delays or concerns
  • Previous psychiatric or psychological diagnoses (anxiety, depression, personality traits)
  • Medication history
  • Any previous autism-related notes
  • Physical health conditions relevant to your assessment

Your GP can send this securely; it's a free service. Ask specifically for records mentioning neurodevelopmental concerns or mental health difficulties.

Psychological Assessment Results

If you've ever had:\p>

  • IQ testing or cognitive assessments
  • Psychometric testing for work or university
  • Previous psychological or psychiatric assessments
  • Dyslexia or other learning difficulty assessments

Bring copies. These provide baseline cognitive information and may reveal patterns relevant to autism diagnosis.

Photos and Videos

Some assessors find old photos or videos helpful:

  • School photos: How you looked and presented compared to peers
  • Videos of yourself: Your movement style, interaction patterns, stimming behaviours
  • Family photos: Context for your development and family dynamics

These are optional but can provide objective information about your presentation.

Employment Records

If autism has affected your work, bring:

  • Performance reviews mentioning interpersonal difficulties, sensory issues, or organisational challenges
  • Occupational health reports
  • Workplace assessments for reasonable adjustments
  • Sick leave records if autism-related (burnout, overwhelm, sensory overload)

Education Documents

If you went to university:

  • Disability support documentation
  • Access arrangements for exams (if you had extra time for focusing difficulties)
  • University counselling or support service records

Create a Personal Timeline

Before your appointment, write down key events and milestones:

  • Childhood: When did you start noticing you were "different"? Were there specific incidents? Did you prefer solitary play? What were your interests?
  • School: Did you struggle socially? Were you bullied? Did you have close friendships? What subjects interested you?
  • Teenage years: How did social demands change? Did you cope differently in different settings?
  • Early adulthood: When did relationships become more difficult? Employment challenges? Burnout or overwhelm?
  • Now: How do your autism traits manifest currently?

This timeline helps you articulate your experiences chronologically during the assessment.

List Your Current Challenges

Write down how autism affects you now. Include specific examples:

  • Social: "I find it hard to initiate conversations. I often don't know what to say. I have one close friend but struggle to maintain other friendships."
  • Sensory: "I'm overwhelmed by fluorescent lights at work. I wear earplugs in busy environments. Certain clothing textures are unbearable."
  • Interests: "I spend 20+ hours weekly on [interest]. I know vast amounts of detail and struggle to stop talking about it."
  • Routines: "I'm distressed by changes to my routine. Unexpected events cause significant anxiety."
  • Executive function: "I struggle to initiate tasks, even ones I want to do. I procrastinate significantly."

Be specific and honest. Don't downplay difficulties.

Identify a Collateral Source

Many clinics ask a family member or close friend to provide information about how you present. This collateral perspective is valuable because:

  • Family members saw you develop and can speak to lifelong patterns
  • Close others notice things you might minimise or not be aware of
  • Your behaviour in assessment may not reflect your typical functioning

Ask someone who:

  • Has known you since childhood (family member is ideal)
  • Has regular contact with you now
  • Understands you well and can speak honestly
  • Is willing to complete questionnaires or speak with the clinician

Brief them on what the clinician might ask so they're prepared.

Prepare Questions for the Clinician

Write down questions you want to ask:

  • What diagnostic criteria are you using (DSM-5 vs ICD-11)?
  • How long is the assessment process?
  • What happens if I'm diagnosed autistic?
  • What support is available after diagnosis?
  • Can I access NHS services with a private diagnosis?
  • What are your qualifications and experience with autism?
  • How do you assess autism in women/non-binary people/people with high support needs?
  • What happens if you don't think I'm autistic?

Think About Accommodation Needs

Let the clinic know about any access needs before your appointment:

  • Sensory needs: Do you need dimmer lighting? Quiet environment? Ability to stim?
  • Communication style: Do you prefer direct communication, written information, or visual aids?
  • Accessibility: Do you need wheelchair access, parking, or extra break time?
  • Neurodivergence accommodations: Do you need extended time, frequent breaks, or a familiar person present?

Most clinics will try to accommodate reasonable requests if you mention them in advance.

Think About Who to Bring

Some people bring a support person to their assessment:

  • Pros: Having a trusted person can reduce anxiety, help you remember to mention important points, and they can provide collateral information
  • Cons: Their presence might make you self-conscious or affect how you present

If you bring someone, discuss in advance what their role will be. Usually they sit with you but don't directly participate unless asked.

Prepare for the Actual Assessment

Before the day:

  • Get good sleep the night before
  • Plan your route and journey so you can arrive calmly
  • Don't take anything stimulating (alcohol, excessive caffeine) the morning of
  • Arrange time off work so you're not stressed
  • Have a plan for after the appointment - you might feel drained

What to bring:

  • All documents gathered above
  • Your personal timeline and challenge list
  • ID and proof of address
  • Any medication list or medical information
  • Phone and charger
  • Water bottle (stay hydrated)
  • Any comfort items (fidget toy, sweater you can change into if cold)

During the assessment:

  • Be honest about your experiences, struggles, and traits
  • Give specific examples rather than generalisations
  • Tell the clinician if you don't understand a question
  • Mention if you're masking or acting - let them know your authentic experience
  • Ask for breaks if you need them
  • Mention sensory needs (too bright, too quiet, need to stim)

Plan Post-Assessment Care

Assessment appointments are emotionally and cognitively demanding. Plan for recovery:

  • Arrange time off work after the appointment
  • Plan something soothing for later (favourite activity, quiet time, being with supportive people)
  • Don't schedule other demanding activities that day
  • Give yourself permission to feel emotions - relief, anxiety, sadness - that might come up

Research the Clinic and Clinician

Before committing, research your chosen clinic:

  • What qualifications do their clinicians have? (Look for psychiatry, psychology, or specific autism training)
  • How much experience do they have with adult autism, particularly in your demographic?
  • What are their diagnostic criteria and process?
  • What do patient reviews say?
  • Do they offer follow-up support after diagnosis?

Use our directory to research clinics in your area. Look for specific mention of adult autism assessment experience.

Use a Checklist

Before your appointment, use this checklist:

  • [ ] Documents gathered (school reports, medical records, etc.)
  • [ ] Personal timeline written
  • [ ] List of current challenges prepared with specific examples
  • [ ] Collateral source identified (if applicable)
  • [ ] Questions for clinician written down
  • [ ] Clinic contacted about accommodation needs
  • [ ] Support person confirmed (if bringing someone)
  • [ ] Post-assessment recovery plan made
  • [ ] Travel route planned
  • [ ] All necessary items packed

Summary

Thorough preparation for autism assessment gives you the best chance of accurate diagnosis and a smooth process. Gather documents showing your lifelong patterns, write down specific examples of how autism affects you, and prepare any accommodation needs. Being organised and honest helps the clinician understand your experiences fully. Choose a reputable, experienced clinic through our directory, ask questions to ensure they're a good fit, and remember that preparation includes taking care of yourself emotionally before and after the assessment. You're taking an important step toward understanding yourself - preparation helps ensure that step is as successful as possible.
